epigraph.text: Look carefully then how you walk, not as unwise but as wise.

content.text:
When two rays share a common endpoint, they form an angle. Angles are 
fundamental to geometry—they appear in every polygon, they determine 
the shapes of triangles, and they help us measure rotation and direction.

content.title: Angle
content.content:
An **angle** is a figure formed by two rays with a 
common endpoint. The rays are called the **sides** 
of the angle, and the common endpoint is called the **vertex**.

content.text:
An angle formed by rays $\overrightarrow{BA}$ and $\overrightarrow{BC}$ 
can be named in several ways:

items: $\angle ABC$ or $\angle CBA$ (vertex in the middle)
$\angle B$ (when there is only one angle at vertex $B$)
$\angle 1$ (using a number placed inside the angle)

content.text:
Angles are measured in **degrees**, denoted by $°$. 
A full rotation is $360°$. The **Protractor Postulate** establishes 
how we measure angles.

content.title: Protractor Postulate
content.content:
Consider a ray $\overrightarrow{OA}$ and a half-plane on one side of 
$\overleftrightarrow{OA}$. Every ray $\overrightarrow{OB}$ with $B$ in 
that half-plane corresponds to exactly one real number between $0$ and 
$180$. The measure of $\angle AOB$ is the absolute value of the 
difference of these numbers.
content.label: Theorem

content.text: The **measure** of angle $ABC$ is written $m\angle ABC$. Note:

content.title: Angle Classifications
content.content:
Angles are classified by their measures:

- An **acute angle** has measure between 
 $0°$ and $90°$.

- A **right angle** has measure exactly 
 $90°$.

- An **obtuse angle** has measure between 
 $90°$ and $180°$.

- A **straight angle** has measure 
 exactly $180°$. Its sides are opposite rays.

content.text: Right angles are marked with a small square at the vertex.

content.title: Congruent Angles
content.content:
Two angles are **congruent** if they have 
the same measure. We write $\angle A \cong \angle B$.

content.text: If $\angle A \cong \angle B$, then $m\angle A = m\angle B$, and vice versa.
Just as segments can be combined, so can angles.

content.title: Angle Addition Postulate
content.content:
If point $D$ lies in the interior of $\angle ABC$, then
\[
 m\angle ABD + m\angle DBC = m\angle ABC
\]
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Using angle addition
content.problem:
Ray $\overrightarrow{BD}$ is in the interior of $\angle ABC$. If 
$m\angle ABD = 3x + 5$, $m\angle DBC = 2x - 3$, and $m\angle ABC = 62°$, 
find $m\angle ABD$ and $m\angle DBC$.
content.solution:
By the Angle Addition Postulate:
\[
 m\angle ABD + m\angle DBC = m\angle ABC
\]
\[
 (3x + 5) + (2x - 3) = 62
\]
\[
 5x + 2 = 62
\]
\[
 x = 12
\]

Therefore, $m\angle ABD = 3(12) + 5 = 41°$ and $m\angle DBC = 2(12) - 3 = 21°$.

Check: $41° + 21° = 62°$

content.title: Angle Bisector
content.content:
An **angle bisector** is a ray that divides 
an angle into two congruent angles.

content.text: If $\overrightarrow{BD}$ bisects $\angle ABC$, then $m\angle ABD = m\angle DBC = \frac{1}{2}m\angle ABC$.

content.title: Using an angle bisector
content.problem:
$\overrightarrow{QS}$ bisects $\angle PQR$. If $m\angle PQS = 4x + 3$ 
and $m\angle SQR = 6x - 9$, find $m\angle PQR$.
content.solution:
Since $\overrightarrow{QS}$ bisects $\angle PQR$, we have 
$m\angle PQS = m\angle SQR$:
\[
 4x + 3 = 6x - 9
\]
\[
 12 = 2x
\]
\[
 x = 6
\]

So $m\angle PQS = 4(6) + 3 = 27°$.

Therefore, $m\angle PQR = 2(27°) = 54°$.

marginNotes.text:
Let your eyes look directly forward... 
Do not swerve to the right or to the left.
